title: Embeddings vs. a Generative LLM for Document Matching: The Engineering Case
tags: embeddings, sentence-transformers, cosine-similarity, llm, vector-search, semantic-search, system-design
content: Resume-to-job-description matching looks like an LLM task: two documents go in, a judgment comes out. We built it without one. The system that shipped uses a 110M-parameter sentence embedding model (all-mpnet-base-v2), cosine similarity, and a deterministic scoring function — no generative model anywhere in the pipeline. This post is the engineering argument for that choice. No war stories, no compliance discussion — just what the embeddings-only architecture buys you over a generative LLM when the task is scoring and ranking documents against each other, and where the trade goes the other way. The Two Architectures Generative approach: for each resume/JD pair, send both documents to an LLM with a prompt like “score this candidate against this job description and explain your reasoning.” The model reads both documents and produces a score and a narrative, per pair, per run. Embeddings approach: at upload time, split each document into sections and embed each section once. At scoring time, compute cosine similarities between stored vectors, combine with an exact skill-overlap score, and emit a composite: flowchart LR subgraph upload["Upload time (once per document)"] D[Document] --> C[Section chunking] --> E["Embed each section<br/>(768-dim, CPU)"] --> V[("Vector store")] end subgraph score["Scoring time (per pair)"] V --> S["Best-match cosine<br/>per JD section"] --> W[Weighted average] K["Skill set overlap<br/>(exact arithmetic)"] --> F W --> F["0.6 × semantic + 0.4 × skill"] end The scoring core is small enough to state in full: # Per JD section, take the best cosine across all candidate sections, # then weight by section importance. Vectors are unit-normalized, # so cosine similarity is a plain dot product. def semantic_score(jd_sections, cand_sections, weights): weighted_sum, total_weight = 0.0, 0.0 for jd_name, jd_vec in jd_sections: best = max(np.dot(jd_vec, cand_vec) for _, cand_vec in cand_sections) w = resolve_weight(jd_name, weights) weighted_sum += best * w total_weight += w return weighted_sum / total_weight overall = 0.6 * semantic_score(...) + 0.4 * skill_score(...) Everything below follows from the structural difference between these two designs. Advantage 1: Determinism An embedding model is a pure function. The same text produces the same vector on every run, so the same resume/JD pair produces the same score today, tomorrow, and after a container restart. There is no temperature, no sampling, no run-to-run variance to explain away. A generative scorer is a distribution, not a function. Even at temperature 0, provider-side model updates and floating-point nondeterminism can shift outputs between runs — and any score that can silently change between identical invocations is a score you cannot regression-test. With embeddings, score(A, B) is as testable as any other function in the codebase: fixtures in, exact expected values out, breaks loudly in CI when someone changes a weight. Advantage 2: Explainability That Is a Derivation, Not a Narrative Ask a generative LLM why it scored a candidate 72 and it will produce a fluent paragraph. That paragraph is a plausible story generated after the score — there is no mechanical guarantee it reflects whatever produced the number. The embeddings pipeline’s explanation is the computation: This JD section matched that resume section at cosine 0.81, with weight 0.35. These four required skills matched; this one is missing; this one matched with a two-year experience gap, applying a 0.70 multiplier. 0.6 × semantic + 0.4 × skill = the score, reproducible to the decimal. Every point in the final number traces to a named similarity or a named skill, and every policy constant (the 60/40 split, the 3× required-skill multiplier, the per-year penalty) sits in code where it can be read, argued about, and changed with a diff. When the scoring policy needs to change, it is a code review — not a prompt-engineering session followed by hopeful re-evaluation. Advantage 3: The Cost Curve Bends the Other Way Ranking is a multiplicative workload. N resumes against M job descriptions is N×M comparisons, and every taxonomy tweak or weight change triggers a re-rank. Generative: every comparison re-reads both full documents through the model. Cost is per-token, per-pair, per-run — 200 resumes × 10 JDs = 2,000 LLM calls, each processing a few thousand tokens, every time you re-rank. Embeddings: each document is embedded exactly once, at upload. A comparison after that is a handful of 768-dimensional dot products — microseconds of CPU. Re-ranking the entire corpus costs effectively nothing, forever. The generative design couples your marginal cost to usage; the embeddings design front-loads a one-time cost per document and makes queries free. For any workload where documents are compared many times (which is what “ranking” means), the curves diverge fast. Advantage 4: Latency and Throughput A generative comparison takes seconds — the model must ingest both documents and autoregressively produce its judgment, one token at a time. Ranking a few hundred candidates serially is minutes; doing it in parallel is a rate-limit negotiation. The embeddings comparison is a memory read plus NumPy dot products. Ranking every active candidate against a JD completes interactively, on 2 vCPU, with no rate limits because there is nothing to rate-limit. Interactive re-ranking changes how the tool gets used: adjusting a section weight and immediately seeing the ranking shift is a feedback loop a batch-of-LLM-calls design cannot offer. Advantage 5: Nothing Leaves the Box The embedding model is ~420 MB of files loaded by a CPU process, baked into the container image at build time. At runtime the system makes zero external calls: no third-party API in the data path, no vendor seeing document contents, no retention policy to review, no dependency on someone else’s uptime or deprecation schedule. The privacy boundary is the deployment boundary — and the same property makes the stack portable to any Docker host, air-gapped included. A generative design — API-based or even self-hosted behind an API layer — puts a network hop and usually a vendor between your documents and your score. Advantage 6: Operational Simplicity Compare what each design asks you to operate: Dimension Generative LLM Embeddings + scoring Score stability Varies run-to-run; drifts on model updates Bit-identical until you change code Explanation Post-hoc narrative Arithmetic derivation Marginal cost per comparison Per-token, every run ~Zero (vectors precomputed) Full re-rank of corpus N×M model calls N×M dot products, interactive Latency per pair Seconds Microseconds Hardware GPU or external API 2 vCPU / 4 GB, CPU-only External dependencies Provider API, quotas, deprecations None at runtime Behavior changes Prompt engineering + re-evaluation Code diff + unit tests Failure modes Hallucinated skills, format drift, refusals Parsing gaps (visible, loggable) The last row deserves a note. The embeddings pipeline fails legibly: if section detection mangles a PDF or a skill alias is missing from the taxonomy, the gap is inspectable in the stored data. A generative scorer fails fluently — inventing a skill match or drifting from the requested output format while sounding entirely confident — and fluent failures are the expensive kind, because nothing looks wrong. Where a Generative LLM Would Win The honest half of the comparison — the trade has real costs: Nuance and negation. Embeddings score “migrated away from Java” uncomfortably close to “expert in Java,” and weigh “used Python in 2015” like “uses Python daily.” A capable LLM reads both correctly. We mitigate with explicit years extraction, but the semantic component stays exposed. Zero-shot flexibility. An LLM handles a new document type, an unusual JD format, or a “compare these on communication skills” request with a prompt change. The embeddings pipeline needs code: chunking rules, taxonomy entries, weight configuration. No curation burden. The skill taxonomy and its aliases require ongoing human gardening. An LLM ships with world knowledge; our pipeline ships with a CRUD admin page. Narrative output. If the product requirement is a written assessment — an actual paragraph a recruiter sends to a hiring manager — you need generation, by definition. The pattern in that list: the LLM wins where the task genuinely requires reading comprehension or writing. It does not win at producing a stable, auditable number — which is what ranking asks for. There is also a middle path worth naming: keep the embeddings pipeline as the ranking engine and add a cross-encoder reranker over the top-N — still deterministic, still local, sharper at the head of the ranking. And if narrative summaries are ever needed, generate them from the structured score breakdown rather than letting a generative model produce the score itself. Generation as a presentation layer over deterministic scoring keeps every advantage above. The Takeaway Choose by output type, not by hype cycle. If the task’s output is prose, you need a generative model. If the output is a number used to order things — matching, ranking, deduplication, recommendation, triage — then a small embedding model plus explicit scoring code gives you determinism, derivable explanations, near-zero marginal cost, interactive latency, an airtight data boundary, and a system you can unit-test. For document matching, the 110M-parameter encoder wasn’t the compromise. It was the correct tool.

title: Deploy a Full App Stack for Nearly Free: What the Major Cloud Providers Actually Give You
tags: azure, aws, gcp, cloud, deployment, free-tier, serverless, containers, database, cicd
content: With AI-assisted coding making it easier than ever to build working apps, the bottleneck has shifted: not building the app, but putting it somewhere other people can actually use it. A solo developer or a small team can now go from idea to working prototype in a weekend. The problem starts on Monday, when they open a cloud provider’s pricing page. Those pages are not designed to help you spend less. They are organized around products, not use cases — dozens of service tiers, interlinked dependencies, pricing calculators that require you to already know what you want. The natural response is to close the tab and either leave the project on localhost or pay for something familiar like a $20/month Heroku equivalent, on the assumption that production-grade deployment just costs money. That assumption is mostly wrong, at least for the first phase of any project. Azure, AWS, and GCP each maintain a set of always-free services — not trials, not credits, not “free for 90 days” promotional offers, but permanent free limits that reset monthly and never expire. They are buried under the marketing, but they exist and they are genuinely generous. A full-stack application — React frontend, REST API backend, relational database, and a CI/CD pipeline — can run inside the free tier of one or more providers at low-to-moderate traffic with a monthly cloud bill of zero. The difficulty is knowing where exactly those limits sit for each layer of your stack. The frontend story is different from the database story. The VM story is different from the serverless story. And the “always free” story is very different from the “12-month trial that becomes a bill you forgot about” story. Cloud providers do not make these distinctions obvious, which is how developers end up surprised by charges on services they thought were free — or avoid the cloud entirely because the pricing looks too complex to navigate. This article maps the free tier limits for every layer of a typical full-stack app — frontend, backend, virtual machines, containers, CI/CD, relational database, NoSQL database, and ML/data analytics — across all three major cloud providers. Each section explains what you actually get, where the limits bite, and what to do when they do. Where the free tier genuinely runs out (primarily relational databases and container registries), the cheapest paid option is included so you know the real cost floor before committing to a stack. Who this is for: developers who have built something and want to ship it; junior-to-mid engineers setting up their first production deployment; and senior engineers who know how to deploy but have never audited what they could be running for free. The numbers in this article are current as of mid-2026 — cloud providers do adjust free tier limits, so the reference links in each table are the authoritative source. A note on free tier types before the numbers: cloud providers use two distinct categories. Always Free means the limit resets monthly and never expires. 12-Month Free means the limit applies to new accounts for the first year only — after that, you pay. The distinction matters. Decisions built on a 12-month free tier that aren’t revisited before expiration are how unexpected bills appear. 1. Frontend — Static Hosting The Problem A React, Vue, or plain HTML/CSS application compiled to a dist/ or build/ folder is a collection of static files. Serving static files is one of the cheapest things a cloud provider can do, and it shows in how generous their free tiers are. The mistake most developers make is defaulting to a full application server — an EC2 instance, a VM, a container — to serve what is essentially a folder of files. You pay for idle compute when a CDN would serve the same files faster and for free. The Solution All three major providers offer static hosting with CDN delivery. GitHub Pages is included as a fourth option — it is the simplest path for public projects and has no cloud account requirement. Provider Service Storage Bandwidth/mo Always Free Ref Azure Static Web Apps 0.5 GB 100 GB ✓ Docs AWS CloudFront + S3 5 GB S3 (12-mo) 1 TB CF: ✓; S3: 12-mo Docs GCP Firebase Hosting 10 GB 360 MB/day ✓ Docs GitHub Pages 1 GB 100 GB ✓ Docs Azure Static Web Apps is the most complete free option for a production app. It includes a built-in serverless API layer (backed by Azure Functions), GitHub Actions CI/CD integration, custom domain support with auto-provisioned SSL, and staging environments — all on the free plan. The 100 GB/month bandwidth limit handles thousands of daily visitors without issue. AWS CloudFront offers 1 TB/month of free data transfer out, permanently — the most generous bandwidth limit of the group. The catch is that S3 (the underlying storage) is only free for 12 months on new accounts. After that, S3 storage for static sites is inexpensive ($0.023/GB/month), but it is no longer free. Firebase Hosting has the largest included storage (10 GB) but its bandwidth allowance — 360 MB per day — is a hard ceiling that a modest traffic spike can hit in hours. It suits apps with steady, low traffic, not anything likely to get a sudden surge. GitHub Pages is the easiest starting point. Push a branch, enable Pages in repository settings, and the site is live. The 1 GB storage limit is tight for image-heavy sites, but 100 GB/month of bandwidth means traffic is not the constraint. Limitations Azure Static Web Apps’ free plan limits you to 10 apps per subscription and restricts API execution time to 45 seconds per invocation. For most apps, neither limit is relevant — but if you are building multiple projects under one Azure account, the 10-app ceiling matters. GitHub Pages is available for public repositories only on free GitHub accounts. It serves static files exclusively — no server-side logic, no API routes, no environment variables at runtime. Firebase’s daily bandwidth cap (360 MB) rather than monthly is unusual. An app that gets mentioned on a popular forum can breach the daily limit within minutes. Budget accordingly or upgrade to the Blaze (pay-as-you-go) plan if the site is externally linked. 2. Backend — Serverless Functions The Problem A REST API or batch job running in a full container or VM wastes most of what it pays for. An API handling 10 requests per second runs for a few milliseconds of compute per request — the other 999 milliseconds of each second, the server sits idle, billing by the hour. Serverless execution inverts this: you pay only for the milliseconds the function actually runs. At low to moderate request volumes, the free tier covers everything. The Solution Serverless functions are where the always-free tiers are most generous. All three providers offer permanently free compute limits large enough to run a production API at modest scale. Provider Service Invocations/mo Compute/mo Always Free Ref Azure Functions 1M 400K GB-s ✓ Docs AWS Lambda 1M 400K GB-s ✓ Docs AWS API Gateway 1M REST calls — ✓ Docs GCP Cloud Functions (1st gen) 2M 400K GB-s ✓ Docs AWS Lambda + API Gateway is the most widely documented serverless stack. Lambda handles function execution; API Gateway exposes it as an HTTP endpoint. Both have permanent 1 million free invocations per month. A typical REST API with 10 endpoints handling 100 requests/day runs under 300,000 invocations per month — comfortably inside the free tier. Lambda also accepts a free Function URL (no API Gateway needed) for simpler use cases. Azure Functions matches Lambda on compute (400,000 GB-seconds) and free invocations (1 million). If your frontend is already on Azure Static Web Apps, Functions is the natural backend companion — the Static Web Apps free plan includes a built-in Functions integration that requires no separate configuration. GCP Cloud Functions (1st gen) offers twice the free invocations (2 million/month) for the same compute budget. One important caveat: Cloud Functions 2nd gen (based on Cloud Run) does not share the same always-free tier as 1st gen. If you use 2nd gen, you are in pay-as-you-go territory from the first invocation. Limitations The GB-seconds metric is a product of memory allocation and execution duration. A function configured with 512 MB of memory that runs for 1 second consumes 0.5 GB-seconds. At 400,000 GB-seconds free per month, that function can run 800,000 times per month — well beyond the 1 million invocation cap. In practice, invocations are usually the binding constraint, not compute time. Cold start latency — the delay when a function initializes after a period of inactivity — affects all three platforms. For interactive APIs, this produces occasional response times of 500ms to 2 seconds on the first request after a quiet period. This is a trade-off of the serverless model, not a platform bug. If consistent sub-100ms response times are required, keep a warmed container (see the next section) or use provisioned concurrency (which is paid). 3. Virtual Machines The Problem Containers and serverless functions handle the majority of backend workloads well — but there are cases where you need the full surface of an operating system. Self-hosted databases, game servers, VPN endpoints, continuous background daemons, legacy applications that cannot be containerized, and anything that requires kernel-level configuration all need a VM. The challenge is that a VM is always-on by definition: unlike a serverless function or a scaled-to-zero container, it runs continuously whether serving traffic or sitting idle, and the billing reflects that. The free tier picture for VMs is thinner than any other category among Azure, AWS, and GCP — all three offer VM free tiers limited to 12 months. Oracle Cloud Infrastructure (OCI) is the exception that makes this section worth covering: it provides the only genuinely always-free VM allocation among major cloud providers, and its ARM Ampere instances are meaningfully more capable than a t2.micro. The Solution Provider Service vCPU RAM Always Free Ref OCI Ampere A1 (ARM) 2 OCPU 12 GB ✓ Docs OCI AMD Micro ×2 1/8 OCPU ea. 1 GB ea. ✓ Docs GCP e2-micro 2 vCPU (shared) 1 GB ✓ US only Docs AWS EC2 t3.micro 2 vCPU 1 GB 12-mo trial Docs Azure B1s 1 vCPU 1 GB 12-mo trial Docs OCI Ampere A1 is the standout option in this category. 2 OCPUs and 12 GB of RAM — configurable across up to 4 instances — is enough to run a small Kubernetes cluster, a self-hosted database, or a backend service with a sidecar. Arm-based workloads are broadly supported by modern Linux distributions and all mainstream server runtimes (Node.js, Python, Go, Java, .NET). The free tier does not expire. In addition to the A1 ARM instances, OCI also provides two Always Free AMD Micro VMs (VM.Standard.E2.1.Micro), each with 1/8 OCPU and 1 GB RAM. These are suitable for lightweight daemons or utility processes, not for serving application traffic. GCP e2-micro is the only always-free x86 VM among the big three cloud providers. One instance per billing account, restricted to US regions (us-central1, us-east1, us-west1). For US-based applications this is rarely a practical limit. For anything requiring a non-US region, the e2-micro free tier is unavailable. AWS t3.micro and Azure B1s are structurally identical in what they offer: 750 hours per month of a single small VM for the first 12 months after account creation. At exactly one instance running 24/7, the 750 hours covers a full month. After 12 months, both cost approximately $7–9/month. These are useful for getting started but are not a long-term free option. Limitations OCI’s Ampere A1 free tier was reduced in June 2026 from 4 OCPUs/24 GB to 2 OCPUs/12 GB for always-free accounts — verify your current allocation in the OCI console if you sized workloads against the old limit. Pay-as-you-go OCI accounts may still receive the higher allocation; the reduction targets accounts on the always-free plan specifically. OCI’s free tier is also subject to availability constraints. Provisioning an A1 instance in high-demand regions — us-ashburn-1 is the most affected — frequently returns “Out of Capacity” errors even for free-tier accounts. Retry at off-peak hours or switch to a less-contested region (eu-frankfurt-1, ap-sydney-1) if provisioning fails repeatedly. VMs do not scale to zero. An idle OCI A1 or GCP e2-micro VM continues to consume its free allocation around the clock. There is no monetary cost, but the capacity is occupied — you cannot bank unused compute or provision a different VM shape while the allocation is held. Delete free VMs you are not actively using rather than leaving them idle; this frees the allocation for a different configuration when you actually need it. 4. Containers — Backend API and Registry The Problem Not every backend fits neatly into a serverless function. Long-running processes, batch jobs, WebSocket servers, ML inference endpoints, and applications with complex startup dependencies are difficult to shoehorn into a stateless function model. Containers are the natural fit — but running a container on a VM means paying for a VM around the clock, even when idle. Container-as-a-service platforms solve this by scaling to zero: the container shuts down when there is no traffic and starts again on the next request. Before any container can run in the cloud, it needs to be stored somewhere. Container registries are the equivalent of npm publish for Docker images — push an image, reference it by tag, pull it at deployment time. Container Registries Provider Service Free Storage Always Free Ref GitHub GHCR (public) Unlimited ✓ Docs AWS ECR Public 50 GB/mo ✓ Docs AWS ECR Private 500 MB 12-mo trial Docs GCP Artifact Registry 0.5 GB ✓ Docs Azure Container Registry — ✗ (Basic ~$5/mo) Docs GitHub Container Registry (GHCR) is the practical default for most projects: unlimited storage for public images, free pulls, and no cloud account required beyond a GitHub account. If your repository is already on GitHub, storing your image in GHCR adds no overhead and eliminates the registry cost entirely. Private GHCR images count against your GitHub Actions storage quota (500 MB on free accounts). AWS ECR Public offers 50 GB/month of free storage for public repositories — enough for dozens of application images at typical sizes. Private ECR storage is free for only 12 months on new accounts. Azure Container Registry has no free tier. The cheapest option is the Basic SKU at approximately $5/month, which includes 10 GB of storage. This is the one unavoidable line item that makes an otherwise free Azure deployment cost something from day one. Container Hosting Scale-to-zero is supported by both GCP Cloud Run and Azure Container Apps, meaning the container shuts down when idle and cold-starts on the next request. AWS has no equivalent always-free hosted container service. Provider Service Compute/mo Requests/mo Always Free Ref GCP Cloud Run 180K vCPU-s + 360K GB-s 2M ✓ Docs Azure Container Apps 180K vCPU-s + 360K GB-s — ✓ Docs AWS App Runner / Fargate — — ✗ Docs GCP Cloud Run is the strongest free offering for containerized backends. 2 million free requests per month with scale-to-zero means a container-based API runs entirely free at low traffic volumes — and you pay only when the container is actually handling requests. The free tier is per billing account and resets monthly. Azure Container Apps matches Cloud Run’s free compute limits exactly (180,000 vCPU-seconds, 360,000 GB-seconds per month) and also scales to zero. The free tier covers a backend API handling several thousand requests per day without cost. AWS has no always-free tier for containerized workloads. App Runner and ECS Fargate are pay-as-you-go from the first second. For AWS users who need containers, Lambda with container image support (Lambda accepts container images up to 10 GB) is the viable alternative that stays inside Lambda’s always-free tier. Limitations Scale-to-zero has the same cold start trade-off as serverless functions. When a Cloud Run or Container Apps instance has scaled to zero and a new request arrives, the container must start before the request is served. For containers with heavier runtimes — Java, .NET with reflection-heavy startup, Python with large import trees — this cold start can exceed 5–10 seconds. Go and Node.js start faster and are better suited to scale-to-zero deployments. The free compute limits on Cloud Run and Container Apps are based on CPU and memory allocation, not wall-clock time. A container allocated 1 vCPU and 512 MB that runs for 100 seconds consumes 100 vCPU-seconds and 50 GB-seconds. At 180,000 vCPU-seconds free per month, that container can handle roughly 1,800 such 100-second runs — at realistic API request sizes (each completing in under a second), the effective free tier covers well over 100,000 requests per month. 5. CI/CD — Deployment Pipelines The Problem A deployment pipeline is the bridge between code and a running application. Without one, every deployment is a manual sequence of steps: build the project, run tests, push a container image, update the hosting environment. Done by hand, this sequence is slow, error-prone, and skipped under deadline pressure — which is exactly when it matters most. CI/CD automates the full path from a git push to a live deployment, and the free tier limits here are among the most practically useful in this entire article, because getting CI/CD wrong costs you time on every single change. The options split into two categories: hosted runners (the CI/CD provider runs the build machine, you pay by the minute) and self-hosted runners (you provision a cloud VM — an OCI A1 instance, a GCP e2-micro, or any other always-free VM from the previous section — install the runner agent, and minutes are unlimited). The runner agent runs as a background service on the VM and polls GitHub or Azure DevOps for queued jobs over outbound HTTPS — no inbound ports, no local machine required. For low-traffic side projects, hosted runners inside the free tier cover everything. For teams or high-volume pipelines, a self-hosted runner on a free cloud VM eliminates the minute constraint entirely. The Solution Hosted CI/CD Provider Service Free Minutes Parallel Jobs Always Free Ref GitHub Actions (public repos) Unlimited 20 ✓ Docs GitHub Actions (private repos) 2,000 Linux 1 ✓ Docs Azure DevOps (private) 1,800 1 ✓ Docs GitLab CI/CD (hosted) 400 1 ✓ Docs CircleCI Free plan ~600 Linux 1 ✓ Docs Bitbucket Pipelines 50 1 ✓ Docs Self-Hosted / On-Premises Option Runs On Minutes Cost Ref GitHub Actions (self-hosted) Any cloud VM Unlimited Free (public); $0.002/min (private) Docs Azure DevOps (self-hosted agent) Cloud VM / server Unlimited Free (1 agent) Docs Azure DevOps Server Windows Server (on-prem) Unlimited Free ≤ 5 users Docs GitLab CE (self-hosted) Cloud VM / server Unlimited Free (OSS) Docs Jenkins Cloud VM / server Unlimited Free (OSS) Docs Woodpecker CI Cloud VM / server Unlimited Free (OSS) Docs GitHub Actions with public repositories is unlimited at zero cost — the most generous CI/CD offer in the industry. Every push, pull request, and scheduled run triggers a fresh Ubuntu, Windows, or macOS runner with no minute counter. For open-source projects or anyone willing to work in public repositories, the free tier is effectively infinite. GitHub Actions on private repositories allocates 2,000 Linux minutes per month on the free plan, with 500 MB of artifact storage. 2,000 minutes covers roughly 66 daily pipeline runs averaging 30 minutes each — sufficient for a solo developer or a small team. macOS runners consume minutes 10× faster than Linux, and Windows runners 2× faster; use Ubuntu runners unless the build specifically requires another OS. Azure DevOps Services grants 1,800 minutes per month (30 hours) and one parallel job for private projects. The practical difference between Azure DevOps and GitHub Actions for private work is narrow. Azure DevOps integrates more tightly with Azure Boards, Azure Repos, and Azure Artifacts if you are running an entirely Microsoft stack, but for a standalone pipeline the free tier limits are nearly identical. Azure DevOps Server (the on-premises version, formerly Team Foundation Server) is free for up to 5 users under the Basic license and runs on any Windows Server machine you already own or manage. Pipelines run on your own hardware, so there are no minute limits. This is the right choice for teams with an on-premises server, air-gapped environments, or anyone who already runs a Windows Server and wants unlimited CI/CD minutes without a cloud account. GitLab CI/CD at 400 minutes per month is tighter than GitHub Actions for private work, but GitLab’s self-hosted Community Edition (free, open source) runs the full GitLab platform — issue tracking, merge requests, container registry, and CI/CD pipelines — on your own server with no minute restrictions. Pairing GitLab CE with an OCI A1 VM gives you a complete DevSecOps platform for $0. Self-hosted runners on GitHub Actions and Azure DevOps are the highest-leverage option for projects that have already provisioned a free cloud VM (OCI A1, GCP e2-micro). The setup is entirely cloud-based: SSH into the VM, download the runner agent, register it with your GitHub organization or repository, and start it as a systemd service. The agent runs permanently in the cloud, polling GitHub over outbound HTTPS for queued jobs — no local machine involved, no inbound firewall rules needed. A single OCI A1 instance (2 OCPU, 12 GB RAM) can comfortably handle concurrent pipelines across several repositories. For public repositories, self-hosted runners are completely free with no platform fee. For private repositories, GitHub began charging a $0.002/min platform fee in March 2026 regardless of where the runner is hosted — on OCI, AWS, GCP, or anywhere else. At $0.002/min, a 10-minute build costs $0.02; a team running 500 builds per month of 10 minutes each pays $10/month in platform fees, even with a free VM underneath. Limitations GitHub Actions’ 2,000 free minutes for private repositories apply to Linux runners only at 1× rate. Windows runners consume minutes at 2× and macOS at 10×. A 10-minute macOS build uses 100 of your 2,000 monthly minutes. Projects that need macOS builds (iOS, cross-platform Electron apps) should route those jobs to a self-hosted macOS machine and reserve hosted runner minutes for Linux-only steps. Azure DevOps Services no longer grants free parallel jobs to new private projects by default — you must request the free grant from Microsoft, which requires manual approval and can take several business days. Check the Azure DevOps parallel jobs page before assuming the 1,800 minutes are immediately available on a new account. Azure DevOps Server requires a Windows Server license, which is not free. If you already run Windows Server for other reasons, the DevOps Server layer is free. If you are standing up infrastructure specifically for CI/CD, GitLab CE or Jenkins on Linux (including OCI’s free A1 ARM instances) is a lower-cost starting point. GitHub Actions self-hosted runners on private repositories are no longer fully free. Starting March 2026, GitHub charges a $0.002/min platform fee for every job minute run on a self-hosted runner in a private repo, regardless of where the runner is hosted. This fee does not apply to public repositories. For private projects where cost is the priority, Azure DevOps self-hosted agents and GitLab CE self-hosted runners remain genuinely unlimited and free. Self-hosted runners introduce a maintenance surface that hosted runners do not have: OS updates, runner version upgrades, disk space management, and secrets management on the host VM. This is manageable on a cloud VM — set up the runner as a systemd service with automatic restarts and a weekly unattended upgrade cron job — but it is real operational overhead that a hosted runner eliminates entirely. 6. Database — Relational (SQL) The Problem Relational databases are where the free tier story gets complicated. A stateless frontend and a serverless backend can scale to zero and cost nothing when idle. A relational database cannot — it runs a persistent server process that must be available to respond to connections, whether or not any traffic exists. That persistent resource costs money. Most free tier options are time-limited trials, but Azure SQL is a notable exception: its free offer is permanent, with monthly usage limits rather than a calendar expiry. The Solution Provider Service Free Limits Always Free Paid Entry Ref Azure Azure SQL (GP Serverless) 100K vCore-s/mo + 32 GB ✓ ~$4.90/mo (Basic) Docs AWS RDS (MySQL / Postgres) 750 hr/mo t3.micro + 20 GB 12-mo trial ~$13–15/mo Docs GCP Cloud SQL — ✗ ~$7–10/mo Docs Supabase Postgres (hosted) 500 MB storage ✓ $25/mo Docs Neon Serverless Postgres 0.5 GB + 190 compute hr ✓ $19/mo Docs Azure SQL Database offers the most generous always-free SQL tier of the three major cloud providers. The free offer runs on the General Purpose Serverless tier — up to 4 vCores, burstable — and resets at the start of each calendar month. Each Azure subscription can hold up to 10 free databases under this offer. The 32 GB data storage and 32 GB backup storage cover a real production workload for most small applications. When the 100,000 vCore-seconds monthly compute budget is exhausted, the database auto-pauses until the first day of the next month (this is the default behavior — you can opt into billing for overage instead if availability matters more than cost). For projects that need a managed relational database outside Azure, Supabase and Neon are the practical always-free options. Both run PostgreSQL — the same engine as AWS RDS for PostgreSQL — so there is no migration friction if you later move to a paid managed option. Supabase bundles Postgres with a REST API, real-time subscriptions, authentication, and storage in a single free project. For small applications, it removes the need for a separate backend service entirely. The 500 MB database limit is tight for data-heavy applications but sufficient for most early-stage projects. Neon runs serverless Postgres that scales to zero, eliminating idle compute cost. 190 free compute hours per month covers intermittent development and low-traffic production use. If you need Azure SQL beyond the free tier limits, Azure SQL Basic DTU at approximately $4.90/month is the cheapest always-on paid entry point — 5 DTUs and 2 GB of storage, no auto-pause risk. AWS RDS db.t3.micro runs $13–15/month after the 12-month trial. GCP Cloud SQL has no meaningful free tier and starts at roughly $7–10/month for the smallest instance. Limitations The Supabase free plan pauses inactive projects after one week of no activity. When a paused project receives its first request, it resumes — but this takes 10–30 seconds. The project can be kept active by scheduling a lightweight ping (a GitHub Actions cron job hitting any endpoint), but this is an extra operational step that a paid plan eliminates. Neon’s 190 free compute hours per month are consumed faster than expected if the database handles queries continuously rather than intermittently. A project running 24/7 with no scale-to-zero exhausts 190 compute hours in roughly eight days. The Azure SQL free tier auto-pauses the database when the monthly 100,000 vCore-second budget is exhausted — all connections are refused until the first of the following month. For a development or low-traffic production database this is often acceptable, but any application that must stay available throughout the month (rather than just during business hours or intermittent use) should either enable the “bill for overage” option or upgrade to Azure SQL Basic DTU (~$4.90/month), which runs without a compute cap. Basic DTU (5 DTUs) is designed for light OLTP workloads with predictable, low concurrency; upgrade to Standard S0 (10 DTUs, ~$15/month) if you see consistent query timeouts under normal load. 7. Database — NoSQL The Problem Not every application needs a relational schema and ACID transactions. For applications that store user-generated content, session data, product catalogs, or event logs — where the shape of records varies and joins are rare — a NoSQL document database often fits better and costs less. The always-free NoSQL tiers are the most generous in the entire database category. The Solution Provider Service Storage Throughput Always Free Ref AWS DynamoDB 25 GB 25 WCU + 25 RCU/mo ✓ Docs Azure Cosmos DB 25 GB 1,000 RU/s ✓ Docs GCP Firestore 1 GB 50K reads + 20K writes/day ✓ Docs MongoDB Atlas M0 512 MB Shared cluster ✓ Docs AWS DynamoDB is the most scalable always-free NoSQL option: 25 GB of storage and enough provisioned throughput to handle 200 million requests per month. The free tier applies to provisioned capacity mode only — on-demand tables are charged per request from the first operation. DynamoDB’s free tier does not expire; it is not a 12-month offer. Azure Cosmos DB offers 1,000 RU/s (request units per second) and 25 GB of storage, permanently. 1,000 RU/s translates to roughly 1,000 point reads or 100 simple queries per second — sufficient for a backend API serving a few hundred concurrent users without bursts. Cosmos DB supports multiple APIs (document, key-value, graph, column-family), making it adaptable to different data models without changing providers. GCP Firestore has the tightest free tier: 50,000 reads and 20,000 writes per day with 1 GB of storage. For apps that batch-read data or cache aggressively, this is workable. For real-time apps that stream frequent small reads — chat, live dashboards — the read limit can be exhausted in minutes by active users. MongoDB Atlas M0 (shared cluster, 512 MB) is a cloud-agnostic option deployable on AWS, Azure, or GCP. It is free indefinitely for a single cluster, and MongoDB’s query model is the most familiar to developers coming from a relational background. Limitations DynamoDB’s access pattern constraints are the most common source of friction when migrating from a relational database. DynamoDB does not support ad-hoc queries across all records without a full table scan — queries must target a partition key. Designing the data model to match access patterns upfront is required. This is not a limitation of the free tier specifically; it is fundamental to how DynamoDB works, and misunderstanding it leads to either expensive scans or a difficult data model redesign later. Cosmos DB’s 1,000 RU/s free limit is shared across all containers (collections) in the free account. If your application creates multiple containers, the 1,000 RU/s is divided among them — not multiplied. A single container doing intensive reads can starve others. Firestore’s daily limits (rather than monthly) mean a single traffic spike can consume the entire day’s quota and return errors for the remainder of the day. Unlike bandwidth limits on hosting services, Firestore returns errors — not degraded responses — when the daily limit is hit. Applications relying on Firestore should implement client-side retry logic and read caching to handle limit exhaustion gracefully. 8. ML & Data Analytics The Problem With AI-assisted development now mainstream, many new applications include an ML component — calling an LLM, generating embeddings, classifying images, or processing data at scale. Each major cloud provider has built a significant ML platform, and the free tier picture here is more uneven than any other layer: the most useful offerings (pre-built model inference APIs) have the thinnest free tiers, while data analytics gets surprisingly generous always-free limits. AI / ML Services Provider Service Free Tier Always Free Ref GCP Gemini API (AI Studio) Flash 2.0: 15 RPM, 1M tok/min ✓ (rate-limited) Docs GCP Vertex AI $300 credit, 90 days ✗ Docs AWS Bedrock No free tier ✗ Docs AWS SageMaker 250 notebook hrs 12-mo trial Docs Azure AI Services 5K transactions/mo 12-mo trial Docs Azure Azure OpenAI $200 credit ✗ Docs GCP’s Gemini API via AI Studio is the only always-free path to a production-grade LLM API among the major cloud providers. Gemini 2.0 Flash at 15 requests per minute and 1 million tokens per minute is sufficient for prototype and low-traffic production use. It is a permanent rate-limited free tier, not a trial. One caveat worth noting: requests on the free tier may be used to improve Google’s models. Switch to a paid Vertex AI endpoint if data privacy requires it. AWS Bedrock and Azure OpenAI have no permanent free tier for inference. New accounts receive credits ($200 for AWS, $200 for Azure) that cover initial experimentation, but inference is pay-as-you-go once credits are exhausted. For cost-sensitive projects, using the Gemini free tier via GCP for LLM calls while deploying other infrastructure on AWS or Azure is a practical hybrid that avoids inference costs entirely. Data Analytics Provider Service Free Queries Free Storage Always Free Ref GCP BigQuery 1 TB processed/mo 10 GB ✓ Docs AWS Athena — ($5/TB scanned) — ✗ Docs Azure Synapse Analytics — — ✗ Docs GCP BigQuery stands out as the only always-free large-scale analytics service among the three providers. 1 TB of free query processing per month covers substantial workloads — querying a 1 GB table 1,000 times, or a 100 GB table 10 times. For analytics-heavy workloads, GCP’s free tier is materially more generous than AWS or Azure. AWS Athena and Azure Synapse both charge from the first query. Athena at $5 per TB scanned adds up quickly for exploratory analysis on large datasets — a common pattern in data science and analytics workflows. Limitations BigQuery’s 1 TB free query limit applies per billing account per month and does not carry over. A single poorly-written query scanning a 2 TB table exhausts double the monthly free allocation in one run. BigQuery offers a “dry run” mode that estimates bytes scanned before executing — use it before running anything on a large table. Gemini’s free tier rate limits (15 requests per minute for 2.0 Flash) are sufficient for development use but will throttle any production application with concurrent users. Burst traffic exceeding the rate limit returns HTTP 429 errors. Applications using the Gemini free tier need client-side retry logic with exponential backoff. The free tier is not appropriate for applications with response-time SLAs or high simultaneous user counts. Putting It All Together The free tier landscape, summarized by layer: Layer Best Free Option Notes Static Frontend GitHub Pages (100 GB/mo bandwidth) Azure Static Web Apps if you need a built-in API Serverless Backend GCP Cloud Functions (2M invocations) AWS Lambda if ecosystem or documentation matters Virtual Machine OCI Ampere A1 (2 OCPU, 12 GB RAM) GCP e2-micro if staying big-three (US regions only) CI/CD GitHub Actions (unlimited on public repos) Self-hosted runner on OCI A1 for unlimited private minutes Containerized Backend GCP Cloud Run (2M req + scale-to-zero) Azure Container Apps matches on compute Container Registry GitHub GHCR (unlimited public) AWS ECR Public (50 GB) for AWS-native stacks SQL Database Azure SQL GP Serverless (100K vCore-s/mo, 32 GB) Supabase / Neon for Postgres; Basic DTU if always-on needed NoSQL Database AWS DynamoDB (25 GB, always free) Azure Cosmos DB (25 GB + 1K RU/s) LLM / ML Inference GCP Gemini 2.0 Flash (15 RPM, always free) No always-free equivalent on AWS or Azure Data Analytics GCP BigQuery (1 TB/mo, always free) No always-free equivalent on AWS or Azure A complete stack — React frontend on GitHub Pages, serverless API on AWS Lambda + API Gateway, and data in DynamoDB — runs entirely within always-free limits. If you need a relational SQL database, Azure SQL’s free offer (100,000 vCore-seconds/month, 32 GB) keeps it within the always-free envelope on Azure; Supabase or Neon work for any stack and also cost nothing. The one cost you will almost certainly not avoid is a domain name. Every provider gives you a free subdomain (*.azurestaticapps.net, *.cloudfunctions.net, *.pages.github.io), but a custom domain runs $10–15/year. That is often the entire annual infrastructure cost for a small production app. The biggest risk is not the free tier limits themselves — it is not knowing when you have left them. Every provider makes it easy to accidentally provision a paid service: a VM left running, an RDS instance with no traffic, a container registry that forgot to prune old images. Set a billing alert at $1 per month in whichever provider’s console you use. If it fires, something slipped outside the free tier and you want to know about it before the bill is $50.

title: VS Code as a Full IDE: Native Tools That Replace Your External Toolchain
content: Most full-stack developers run three to five external tools alongside VS Code — Postman for API testing, GitHub Desktop for git, a dedicated test runner in a separate terminal, and a formatter hooked into a pre-commit script. Each context switch carries a cost: broken focus, mismatched environments, and configuration that lives outside the project repository. VS Code’s native configuration layer eliminates most of that overhead — but only if you know where to look. This article covers six native tools and configurations that replace common external dependencies, reduce context switching, and keep your entire development environment version-controlled inside .vscode/. The configurations cover C#, Python, Node.js, and React, though the patterns apply broadly across any full-stack combination. 1. Code Formatting and Linting (ESLint & Prettier) The Problem Without editor-integrated formatting, styling inconsistencies accumulate silently. Developers format on different schedules — some before commits, some never — and CI linting failures become a routine part of the pull request cycle. When formatters run as a separate manual step or a pre-commit hook, they’re easy to skip, misconfigure, or forget entirely when onboarding a new machine. The Solution VS Code’s formatOnSave and codeActionsOnSave settings integrate formatting and linting directly into the save cycle. Every file is cleaned the moment it’s written, before it reaches staging, review, or CI. This removes the “I forgot to format” class of PR comments permanently. Install the ESLint and Prettier - Code Formatter extensions, then add the following to .vscode/settings.json: { "editor.formatOnSave": true, "editor.codeActionsOnSave": { "source.fixAll.eslint": "explicit" }, "editor.defaultFormatter": "esbenp.prettier-vscode", "[csharp]": { "editor.defaultFormatter": "ms-dotnettools.csharp" }, "[python]": { "editor.defaultFormatter": "ms-python.black-formatter" } } With this in place: JavaScript, TypeScript, and HTML/CSS are formatted automatically on save via Prettier. ESLint auto-fixes violations (unused variables, bad imports, rule infractions) on save. C# files are formatted according to Roslyn/OmniSharp guidelines. Python files are formatted using Black, the de facto standard in modern Python projects. Because this configuration lives in .vscode/settings.json and is committed to the repository, every developer on the team gets identical formatting behavior on clone — no separate formatter setup required. Limitations This approach is VS Code-specific. Developers using JetBrains IDEs, Neovim, or Emacs need equivalent formatter configurations in their own editors — the .vscode/ directory does nothing for them. For cross-editor teams, pairing this with a .editorconfig file and project-level ESLint/Prettier config files (eslint.config.js, .prettierrc) ensures formatting rules are enforced regardless of editor. The VS Code settings layer then becomes a convenience on top of the project-level enforcement, not a replacement for it. 2. Unified Debugging with tasks.json and launch.json The Problem A typical manual debug session in a multi-language project looks like this: open a terminal, run dotnet build, wait for compilation, switch back to the editor, open the Run and Debug panel, select the right configuration, and attach the debugger. That sequence repeats every time you make a change worth testing under the debugger — several times an hour in active development. Each step is a context switch, and any one of them can be skipped or done out of order (debugging stale binaries is a common, frustrating result). The problem compounds across languages. A full-stack developer might need to start a .NET backend, a Node.js API layer, and a React frontend in the correct order, each with its own pre-launch requirements. Doing this manually across multiple terminal tabs is error-prone and slows down every iteration cycle. The Solution VS Code’s .vscode/tasks.json and .vscode/launch.json encode the entire pre-launch sequence as a first-class IDE concern. The preLaunchTask field in a launch configuration chains tasks — format, lint, build — before the debugger attaches. Press F5, and the editor handles compilation, quality checks, and debugger attachment in the correct order automatically. Each language has a distinct pre-launch concern. The configurations below address those differences directly. C# / .NET Core C# requires compilation before debugging. The pre-launch sequence here runs dotnet format first, then dotnet build, so the binary is both up-to-date and clean before the debugger attaches. .vscode/tasks.json { "version": "2.0.0", "tasks": [ { "label": "format-csharp", "command": "dotnet", "type": "process", "args": [ "format", "${workspaceFolder}/Backend/Backend.csproj" ], "problemMatcher": [] }, { "label": "build-csharp", "command": "dotnet", "type": "process", "args": [ "build", "${workspaceFolder}/Backend/Backend.csproj", "/property:GenerateFullPaths=true", "/consoleloggerparameters:NoSummary" ], "dependsOn": [ "format-csharp" ], "problemMatcher": "$msCompile" } ] } .vscode/launch.json { "version": "0.2.0", "configurations": [ { "name": ".NET Core Launch (Backend)", "type": "coreclr", "request": "launch", "preLaunchTask": "build-csharp", "program": "${workspaceFolder}/Backend/bin/Debug/net8.0/Backend.dll", "args": [], "cwd": "${workspaceFolder}/Backend", "stopAtEntry": false, "console": "internalConsole" } ] } Node.js (Express Server) Node.js with TypeScript doesn’t compile to native binaries, but it does require a lint pass and a TypeScript transpilation step (npm run build) before the output JavaScript is safe to run under the debugger. Linting before build catches import errors and type violations that would otherwise surface as runtime exceptions mid-session. .vscode/tasks.json { "version": "2.0.0", "tasks": [ { "label": "lint-node", "type": "npm", "script": "lint", "problemMatcher": [] }, { "label": "build-node", "type": "npm", "script": "build", "dependsOn": [ "lint-node" ], "problemMatcher": [] } ] } .vscode/launch.json { "version": "0.2.0", "configurations": [ { "name": "Node.js: Express Server", "type": "node", "request": "launch", "program": "${workspaceFolder}/server/index.js", "preLaunchTask": "build-node", "skipFiles": [ "<node_internals>/**" ], "cwd": "${workspaceFolder}/server" } ] } React (Frontend) React’s development server is a long-running background process, not a one-shot build. The challenge is that the Chrome debugger cannot attach until the dev server has fully compiled and is serving on its port. The isBackground flag combined with beginsPattern and endsPattern tells VS Code to wait for the “Compiled successfully!” signal before launching Chrome — preventing the race condition where Chrome opens before the bundle is ready. .vscode/tasks.json { "version": "2.0.0", "tasks": [ { "label": "start-react-server", "type": "npm", "script": "start", "isBackground": true, "problemMatcher": { "pattern": { "regexp": "^.*$" }, "background": { "activeOnStart": true, "beginsPattern": "Starting the development server...", "endsPattern": "Compiled successfully!" } } } ] } .vscode/launch.json { "version": "0.2.0", "configurations": [ { "name": "React: Chrome Debugger", "type": "chrome", "request": "launch", "url": "http://localhost:3000", "webRoot": "${workspaceFolder}/frontend", "preLaunchTask": "start-react-server" } ] } Python Python is interpreted, so there is no compilation step. The pre-launch concern is code quality: running ruff check before the session starts catches style violations and import errors without adding meaningful latency. The launch configurations below cover both script-level debugging and Flask application debugging. .vscode/tasks.json { "version": "2.0.0", "tasks": [ { "label": "lint-python", "type": "shell", "command": "ruff check ${fileDirname}", "problemMatcher": [] } ] } .vscode/launch.json { "version": "0.2.0", "configurations": [ { "name": "Python: Current File", "type": "debugpy", "request": "launch", "program": "${file}", "console": "integratedTerminal", "justMyCode": true }, { "name": "Python: Flask App", "type": "debugpy", "request": "launch", "module": "flask", "env": { "FLASK_APP": "app.py", "FLASK_ENV": "development" }, "args": [ "run", "--no-debugger", "--no-reload" ], "jinja": true, "justMyCode": true } ] } Limitations This configuration is editor-bound. Developers not using VS Code cannot benefit from it, and in polyglot teams this can create a two-tier developer experience. Complex monorepos with nested projects can also make preLaunchTask chains brittle — a task defined in a root tasks.json may not resolve paths correctly when the debugger target is in a deeply nested subdirectory. In those cases, workspace-scoped .vscode/ directories per sub-project are necessary. Additionally, the isBackground pattern used for React is sensitive to changes in the dev server’s console output. If the endsPattern string changes across versions of Create React App or Vite, the debugger will hang waiting for a signal that never arrives. When migrating bundlers, re-validate the pattern against the new tool’s actual output. 3. Native NPM Scripts Runner The Problem Running package.json scripts from the terminal is straightforward but fragmented. In a monorepo or multi-project workspace, developers must remember which terminal tab corresponds to which project, which scripts exist, and the exact script names. New team members frequently waste time reading package.json files to discover what scripts are available — or worse, they run the wrong script in the wrong directory. The Solution VS Code’s built-in NPM Scripts Explorer surfaces all package.json scripts from every project in the workspace in a single panel. Scripts can be run, debugged, or stopped with a single click — no terminal navigation required. Enable it in .vscode/settings.json: { "npm.enableScriptExplorer": true, "npm.exclude": "**/node_modules/**" } Once enabled, an NPM SCRIPTS accordion appears at the bottom of the File Explorer sidebar. Every script from every detected package.json in the workspace is listed with clickable run and debug buttons. For keyboard-driven workflows, the Command Palette (Cmd+Shift+P / Ctrl+Shift+P) approach also works: Tasks: Run Task → npm: <script-name> Limitations The NPM Scripts Explorer is a discovery and convenience tool, not a process manager. It does not show whether a script is currently running, does not support restarting processes, and does not aggregate logs from multiple simultaneous scripts into a single view. For projects where multiple long-running scripts need to run concurrently (e.g., a backend watcher, a frontend dev server, and a test watcher), a dedicated tool like tmux or VS Code’s own compound launch configurations offers better process visibility. 4. Integrated Unit Testing via Test Explorer The Problem Running tests in a separate terminal and then returning to the editor to fix failures is a two-step feedback loop. Identifying which test failed, navigating to the correct file and line, and then re-running just that test requires manual coordination between tools. Coverage data typically lives in a separate HTML report that must be opened in a browser. Debugging a failing test — stepping through it with a debugger — usually requires writing a separate debug script or using a test runner flag that most developers don’t bother with. The Solution VS Code’s native Test Explorer (the beaker icon in the sidebar) provides a unified interface for discovering, running, debugging, and inspecting test results directly inside the editor. Install the appropriate language-specific extension, add minimal configuration, and test results appear inline next to the code they cover. Language / Framework Extension Configuration C# (.NET) C# Dev Kit / .NET Test Explorer Native detection of xUnit, NUnit, MSTest Node.js / React Jest / Vitest (by Vitest) "vitest.enable": true Python Python (Microsoft) Pytest or Unittest via settings The critical capability that separates this from running tests in a terminal is in-editor debugging: place a breakpoint inside a test function, click the debug button next to that test in the Explorer, and the debugger attaches for that single test case. This makes diagnosing assertion failures and unexpected exceptions significantly faster than print-debugging or log analysis. Python Pytest (.vscode/settings.json) { "python.testing.pytestArgs": [ "tests" ], "python.testing.unittestEnabled": false, "python.testing.pytestEnabled": true } Node.js / React Jest (.vscode/settings.json) { "jest.disabledWorkspaceFolders": [], "jest.enableCodeLens": true, "jest.shell": "/bin/zsh" } Limitations Test Explorer extensions vary significantly in stability and feature coverage. The Jest extension for VS Code has historically had configuration sensitivity issues — particularly with projects using custom Jest configurations, moduleNameMapper overrides, or non-standard project structures. Vitest’s extension is newer and more reliable for modern setups but lacks some of the Jest extension’s code lens features. For C#, the .NET Test Explorer requires the C# Dev Kit, which is a Microsoft-licensed extension and may not be available in all VS Code forks (e.g., VSCodium). Coverage highlighting inside the editor also depends on the extension generating coverage data in a format it understands — not all test runner configurations produce this automatically. 5. Built-in Git & Source Control Integration The Problem Git CLI is powerful and portable, but it introduces friction during high-velocity development cycles. Resolving a three-way merge conflict in the terminal requires running git diff --cc to view both sides of the conflict, editing the file manually to remove conflict markers, and then staging and committing. Stashing work in progress requires remembering the right combination of git stash push -u -m "description". For developers primarily focused on writing code rather than mastering git internals, these friction points accumulate. The Solution VS Code’s native Source Control panel provides a graphical interface for the full git lifecycle without requiring a third-party tool. Staging, committing, pushing, pulling, stashing, branching, and conflict resolution are all accessible from a single sidebar panel. VS Code native Source Control panel showing changes, staging, stashing, and push menus. The most significant productivity gain is in conflict resolution. When a merge produces conflicts, VS Code opens a dedicated Merge Editor — a three-pane view showing the current branch, the incoming branch, and a merged result pane. Accepting changes from either side, or combining both, is a single click. This is materially faster and less error-prone than manually editing conflict markers in a terminal. Key workflows in the Source Control panel: Cloning: Cmd+Shift+P → Git: Clone → paste repository URL. VS Code clones and prompts to open the folder immediately. Staging & Committing: Click + next to files in the Changes list to stage. Type the commit message in the input box and press Cmd+Enter / Ctrl+Enter. Pushing & Pulling: Sync Changes pushes local commits and fetches upstream changes in one operation. Stashing: ... menu → Stash → Stash (Include Untracked). Restore via ... → Stash → Pop Stash. Merging & Conflict Resolution: Git: Merge Branch... from the Command Palette. Conflicts open automatically in the Merge Editor. Limitations The graphical Source Control panel is designed for linear, day-to-day git workflows. Complex operations — interactive rebase, cherry-picking across multiple commits, bisecting, or reflog recovery — remain significantly easier in the CLI or in a dedicated git GUI like GitKraken or Sourcetree. Attempting these operations through the Command Palette in VS Code often requires multiple steps where a single CLI command would suffice. The Source Control panel also does not give a visual overview of branch topology or commit graph. For repositories with active parallel branches or long release histories, a tool like GitLens (VS Code extension) or a standalone git GUI provides substantially better visibility into branch relationships. 6. REST Client with Multi-Environment & Azure AD Config The Problem Postman and similar GUI tools solve API testing, but they introduce a critical separation: the API test collection lives outside the codebase. When the API changes, the tests must be updated in Postman separately — and that update may or may not happen. Collections stored in Postman’s cloud are also subject to their team plan pricing and are not automatically synchronized with the repository. For teams practicing infrastructure-as-code or treating their .http files as executable documentation, this separation is a liability. The Solution The REST Client extension (by Huachao Mao) allows HTTP requests to be written in plain .http files and executed directly inside VS Code. These files live in the repository alongside the code they test, are version-controlled, and can reference environment variables defined in .vscode/settings.json. The result is an API test suite that is always in sync with the backend it targets. Step 1: Define environments in .vscode/settings.json { "rest-client.environmentVariables": { "$shared": { "tenantId": "your-azure-ad-tenant-id", "clientId": "your-azure-ad-client-app-id" }, "local": { "baseUrl": "http://localhost:5000", "audience": "api://localhost-backend" }, "staging": { "baseUrl": "https://api-staging.example.com", "audience": "https://api.example.com" }, "production": { "baseUrl": "https://api.example.com", "audience": "https://api.example.com" } } } Step 2: Write .http files with Azure AD authentication The REST Client supports named requests, allowing the token response from one request to be captured and reused as a variable in subsequent requests. This makes OAuth2 client credentials flows — common in Azure AD / Entra ID protected APIs — straightforward to automate: # @name getAADToken POST https://login.microsoftonline.com//oauth2/v2.0/token HTTP/1.1 Content-Type: application/x-www-form-urlencoded grant_type=client_credentials &client_id= &client_secret= &scope=/.default ### Cache the Token Variable @authToken = ### Secured API Request GET /api/v1/users HTTP/1.1 Authorization: Bearer Content-Type: application/json Switch environments using the environment selector in the VS Code status bar. All `` references update automatically. Recommended file organization: Structure .http files to mirror the API’s controller layout — one file per controller or resource, grouped under a shared directory (e.g., api-tests/users.http, api-tests/orders.http). Within a file, separate individual requests with ###, which the REST Client treats as a request delimiter. This allows multiple variations of the same endpoint (e.g., valid request, missing auth header, malformed payload) to coexist in a single file without ambiguity. api-tests/ auth.http users.http orders.http products.http This structure scales cleanly as the API grows and makes it immediately clear which .http file covers which part of the backend. For the full feature set — request history, cookies, GraphQL support, and response variable chaining — see the REST Client extension documentation. Limitations The REST Client is a capable replacement for Postman in solo and small-team contexts, but it lacks several features that larger teams rely on. There is no shared cloud workspace — collaboration requires committing .http files to the repository and ensuring team members have the extension installed. There are no mock server capabilities, no automated test runner that produces structured pass/fail reports, and no GUI for browsing response history across sessions. For teams that need Postman’s collaboration features, collection runner, or Newman CI integration, the REST Client is a complement rather than a full replacement. Note on secrets: The clientSecret variable in the .http file above should never be committed to the repository. Store it as a user-level environment variable or reference it from a .env file that is included in .gitignore. Summary: A Version-Controlled Development Environment The six configurations above share a common principle: development environment setup belongs in the repository, not in each developer’s head. By committing .vscode/tasks.json, .vscode/launch.json, .vscode/settings.json, and .http files alongside the application code, the workspace becomes self-documenting and immediately reproducible. The practical outcomes: Reduced onboarding time: A developer who clones the repository gets a fully configured formatter, debugger, test runner, and API client on first open — no separate setup documentation required. Eliminated context switching: Building, debugging, testing, and API validation happen inside a single editor window. External tools are no longer a requirement for the core development loop. Consistent code quality: Formatting and linting happen on every save across every developer’s machine, removing a class of review comments and CI failures. Version-controlled API tests: .http files evolve with the API they test, in the same commit, reviewed in the same pull request. These configurations work best as a team-wide commitment. A .vscode/ directory that one developer maintains and others ignore provides none of the onboarding or consistency benefits. The highest-leverage first step is committing a baseline settings.json with formatOnSave enabled — it is the lowest-friction change with the broadest impact across every language in the stack.

title: Engineering at Scale: Notes on Architecture, Cloud, and AI
content: Welcome. This is where I document the engineering decisions, architectural patterns, and research that shape the systems I build. The writing here is practical and opinionated — drawn from real-world delivery, not theory alone. About Me I am a Full-Stack Software Architect focused on designing and delivering scalable, resilient, cloud-native systems across AWS, GCP, and Azure. My work spans the full lifecycle — from architecture and design through implementation, deployment, and operation at scale. With an M.Tech in Data Science, I work at the intersection of enterprise software architecture and modern data systems. My current focus and ongoing research center on: Artificial Intelligence & Machine Learning (AI/ML) Generative AI (GenAI) & Large Language Models (LLMs) Scalable Distributed Systems & Multi-Cloud Architecture What to Expect I publish technical articles, architectural guides, and research notes grounded in production experience: System Design & Cloud Architecture: Proven patterns and trade-off analysis for building multi-cloud solutions that scale. AI & Data Engineering: In-depth treatments of machine learning workflows, data pipeline engineering, and applied GenAI. Modern Web & Full-Stack Development: Practical guidance and best practices for building robust, maintainable applications. Expect rigor over hype and depth over breadth. The first articles are on the way.
